does: This paper introduces a novel rule-breaking question answering (RBQA) challenge set designed to evaluate how well AI systems can predict human moral judgments in cases involving permissible exceptions to rules. The authors develop MoralCoT, a chain-of-thought prompting strategy that integrates theories from moral psychology and cognitive science into large language model reasoning. When applied to state-of-the-art LLMs, MoralCoT achieves a 6.2% F1 improvement over seven existing models, demonstrating that structured moral reasoning aligned with human psychological theories can better capture judgments about when rules should be broken in novel or unusual situations.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in chain-of-thought prompting and interpretability research that show how eliciting intermediate reasoning steps can improve LLM performance on complex tasks. It connects to a broader tradition in moral psychology and philosophy examining rule-breaking, moral flexibility, and contextual judgment in human decision-making. The paper engages with research on AI safety that emphasizes the importance of value alignment and understanding human preferences, extending prior work on using LLMs for moral reasoning by grounding the approach in cognitive science theories rather than relying solely on empirical scaling of model capacity.
enables: This research opens directions for more nuanced alignment of AI systems with human moral reasoning, particularly for safety-critical applications where systems must navigate exceptions and contextual reasoning. It provides a foundation for building evaluation benchmarks that test moral reasoning flexibility rather than rigid rule-following. The work enables future research on improving AI interpretability in moral domains, developing better training methods that incorporate cognitive science insights, and creating more robust human-AI collaboration frameworks where systems can explain their reasoning about rule-breaking decisions in ways humans can understand and evaluate.
